original_hypothesis.title: Kernel can be permanently bricked by setting executor/admin to address(0) via executeAction (no zero-address guard)
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: Kernel.executeAction allows Actions.ChangeExecutor and Actions.ChangeAdmin without validating target_ != address(0). If set to zero, no one can execute privileged lifecycle actions or manage roles, effectively freezing governance and potentially preventing module upgrades/fixes.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: This is a concrete code defect with immediate protocol-level DoS impact if accidentally invoked; it’s also a relevant attack in the case of compromised executor/admin wanting to permanently sabotage recovery.

proof_of_signal.lines: executeAction: ChangeExecutor/ChangeAdmin branches set executor/admin = target_ without checks
proof_of_signal.pattern: Missing non-zero validation on critical governance addresses

preconditions: Action is ChangeExecutor or ChangeAdmin with target_=address(0)

analysis_questions: Is there any recovery path if executor/admin becomes zero (e.g., through migration)?
Do deployment scripts or operational runbooks prevent this at tooling level?
validation_requirements: Unit test: set executor to zero and show executeAction becomes unusable
Assess whether any module/policy can still function safely without governance

original_hypothesis.preliminary_attack_scenario: Compromised executor sets admin and/or executor to address(0) to permanently prevent remediation actions (upgrades, deactivations, role changes).
prerequisites: Executor key compromise or malicious executor (out-of-scope for HIGH realism theft, but DoS is on-chain once authorized)

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Indirect but significant: inability to patch/upgrade modules; could lock protocol in broken state
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Low without social recovery/migration to new deployments

original_hypothesis.test_coverage_gap: Missing tests for governance address invariants

validation_result.mechanism: missing zero-address guard on critical governance addresses
validation_result.impact_summary: Kernel.executeAction allows setting executor or admin to address(0) without validation, permanently bricking all governance operations including module installs, policy activations, role grants, and upgrades.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: The executeAction function in Kernel.sol handles Actions.ChangeExecutor and Actions.ChangeAdmin by directly assigning target_ to executor/admin state variables without any validation. Unlike the other action types (InstallModule, UpgradeModule, ActivatePolicy, DeactivatePolicy, MigrateKernel) which all call ensureContract(target_), the ChangeExecutor and ChangeAdmin branches perform no checks at all. If target_ is address(0), the assignment succeeds. Once executor is address(0), the onlyExecutor modifier will reject all callers since no EOA or contract can have msg.sender == address(0) in the EVM. Similarly, if admin is address(0), onlyAdmin will reject all grantRole/revokeRole calls. There is no recovery mechanism - no backdoor, no timelock, no multi-sig override. The kernel becomes permanently non-functional for governance operations.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: The defect is clearly visible in Kernel.sol's executeAction function. Lines for ChangeExecutor and ChangeAdmin simply assign `executor = target_` and `admin = target_` respectively, with zero validation. All other action branches include at minimum `ensureContract(target_)`. The constructor also lacks zero-address checks but that's a deployment-time concern. The runtime vulnerability via executeAction is the primary defect.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: Neither branch validates that target_ != address(0). Setting executor to address(0) makes the onlyExecutor modifier reject all callers permanently, since msg.sender can never equal address(0). Same applies to admin and onlyAdmin. No other action branches (InstallModule, UpgradeModule, etc.) have this gap because they all call ensureContract(target_) which would revert on address(0).

calling_sequence: 2. onlyExecutor modifier passes since msg.sender == current executor
4. executor = target_ (address(0)) is assigned with no validation
6. All subsequent calls to executeAction revert because onlyExecutor requires msg.sender == address(0), which is impossible
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: The entry is executeAction with onlyExecutor modifier. The modifier checks `msg.sender != executor` and reverts if true. Once executor = address(0), this check becomes `msg.sender != address(0)` which is always true for any real caller, permanently locking out all executeAction calls. The same logic applies to admin via onlyAdmin for grantRole/revokeRole.

config_validation.deployment_evidence: Deployment scripts (DeployKernel.s.sol) call _deployKernel(salt) from BaseDeploy base which is not provided. However, even if deployment uses valid addresses, the runtime vulnerability remains - any future executeAction call with address(0) target would brick the kernel. No deployment-time mitigation can prevent a runtime governance action.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: The attack is straightforward: a compromised executor (or an executor making an accidental mistake) calls executeAction(Actions.ChangeExecutor, address(0)). The transaction succeeds. From that point forward, no one can call executeAction, meaning no module installations, upgrades, policy activations/deactivations, or kernel migrations can occur. If admin is also zeroed (or was already zeroed), no role grants/revocations can happen either. The protocol governance is permanently frozen. This requires only the executor key and a single transaction.
validation_result.impact_assessment: MEDIUM-HIGH severity. While exploitation requires a compromised or erroneous executor, the consequences are permanent and protocol-wide: inability to upgrade modules, activate/deactivate policies, manage roles, or migrate the kernel. Active rentals would continue to function, but any bugs or needed changes would be unfixable. The only recovery would be deploying an entirely new protocol instance, which would not help with existing rental state locked in the old modules.
recommended_mitigations: Add require(target_ != address(0)) checks in the ChangeExecutor and ChangeAdmin branches of executeAction
Consider adding a two-step ownership transfer pattern (propose + accept) for executor and admin changes to prevent accidental or malicious transfers to invalid addresses
Add zero-address validation in the Kernel constructor as well
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy Kernel with valid executor and admin addresses. 2. Call executeAction(Actions.ChangeExecutor, address(0)) from the executor. 3. Verify the call succeeds. 4. Attempt any executeAction call and verify it reverts with Kernel_OnlyExecutor. 5. Repeat for ChangeAdmin and verify grantRole/revokeRole revert with Kernel_OnlyAdmin.
uncertainties: BaseDeploy script source is not provided, so constructor-level zero-address handling during deployment cannot be fully verified
additional_testing_needed: Unit test confirming executeAction(ChangeExecutor, address(0)) succeeds
Unit test confirming all subsequent executeAction calls revert after executor is zeroed
Unit test confirming grantRole/revokeRole revert after admin is zeroed
Integration test showing protocol becomes unrecoverable after both executor and admin are zeroed
